does: This paper develops an information-theoretic framework to quantify superposition in neural networks by measuring the effective degrees of freedom in a representation. The authors apply Shannon entropy to sparse autoencoder (SAE) activations to compute the minimum number of neurons required for interference-free feature encoding, which they call the number of virtual neurons the network simulates. They validate their metric against ground truth in toy models, apply it to detect superposition patterns in algorithmic tasks, track how dropout affects superposition levels across layers, and analyze developmental dynamics during training. The metric reveals systematic layer-wise patterns that align with prior work on intrinsic dimensionality and captures sharp transitions in feature learning.
connects: This work builds on longstanding research into feature superposition and distributed representations in neural networks, addressing a gap in interpretability methods by formalizing measurement of a phenomenon previously observed qualitatively. It connects to sparse autoencoders as tools for discovering interpretable features and measuring them, extending recent interpretability work that uses SAEs to decompose network activations. The information-theoretic foundation draws on classical work connecting neural representations to information theory and intrinsic dimensionality. It also relates to adversarial robustness literature, which has hinted that superposition creates vulnerability by forcing feature interference, and builds on empirical studies of representation geometry in models like Pythia that characterize how networks organize information across layers during training.
enables: This metric enables more principled investigation of the superposition-interpretability tradeoff and could guide architectural or training decisions that modulate superposition levels. By quantifying superposition as lossy compression, the framework opens research into whether networks can be trained to reduce harmful interference while maintaining performance, potentially improving both interpretability and robustness. The connection to adversarial vulnerability suggests the metric could serve as a probe to understand which layers are most vulnerable to attack due to high interference. Future work could use this measure to study whether larger models exhibit different superposition dynamics, investigate whether training objectives can be modified to control superposition, or develop methods to recover individual features from superposed representations with bounded error.
